author: Natoli Lemessa
title: Why the Case Against Israel as “Genocidal” Collapses Under Scrutiny
subtitle: Few words in international law carry more weight than “genocide.” It is the ultimate crime, the attempt to wipe out an entire people. The accusation now leveled at Israel over Gaza is not only explosive; it’s meant to frame every civilian death as part of an extermination plan. Yet once the rhetoric is stripped away, the case against Israel collapses — legally, factually, and logically.
content:
Genocide Has a Legal Definition, Not a Hashtag

The 1948 Genocide Convention doesn’t criminalize “wars that cause devastation” or even “wars with reckless civilian harm.” It criminalizes acts carried out with the specific intent to destroy a national, ethnic, racial, or religious group, as such.

That intent is the steepest hill in international law. It is why the International Court of Justice (ICJ), in its provisional rulings, did not declare Israel guilty of genocide. Instead, the ICJ issued interim measures to ensure humanitarian access and to reduce harm — serious orders, but not a judgment on the merits. In fact, the Court pointedly refused to order Israel to halt its military operations, something it has done in other cases where genocidal intent seemed clearer.

This isn’t a defense of every strike Israel has carried out. It’s a reminder: genocide is not shorthand for “war we dislike.”

The Quotes Game: Rhetoric vs. Policy

Critics point to inflammatory statements from Israeli ministers: talk of “flattening Gaza,” “erasing” Hamas territory, or cutting off utilities. Such rhetoric is ugly and damaging. But legal cases are not won on soundbites.

If overheated wartime statements equaled genocide, the Allies would have been branded genocidal for promising to “bomb Germany back into the Stone Age.” Courts judge intent by policy and operations, not by a minister’s microphone moment.

Israel’s policy record shows something different: evacuation warnings, coordination mechanisms with UN aid agencies, and even military discipline in cases of misidentification. That evidence points to a state fighting a brutal war, not orchestrating extermination.

The Starvation Claim: More Complex Than a Slogan

One of the most charged allegations is that Israel is using starvation as a weapon of war. The famine warnings are real — but the causes are multiple.

  • Crossings: After Israel seized the Gazan side of Rafah, Egypt kept the gate closed in protest, cutting off a major artery of aid. That’s not under Israeli control.

  • Distribution chaos: UN reports themselves describe aid convoys looted by gangs, diverted by armed groups, and swarmed by desperate civilians. What enters Gaza often doesn’t reach the people most in need.

  • Alternative routes: Aid has entered by land, air, and sea. Imperfectly, unevenly — but entry exists. If the policy were to starve Gaza, these routes wouldn’t exist at all.

In short: famine risk is real, but famine intent is not proven.

The Death Toll: Tragedy Without Clarity

The numbers themselves are deeply politicized. Most counts originate from Gaza’s Health Ministry — an organ of Hamas — and are later echoed by the UN with caveats.

In May 2024, the UN had to cut its own tally of women and children killed almost in half, while keeping the overall death toll similar. That doesn’t mean people aren’t dying; it means the data is too volatile to hang a genocide conviction on.

If your spreadsheet shifts by 50% in a week, it’s a weak foundation for the heaviest crime on earth.

Journalists and Aid Convoys: Tragedy Isn’t Proof of Policy

This has been the deadliest war for journalists in decades. The Committee to Protect Journalists calls the numbers unprecedented. Yet CPJ itself admits most were killed in bombardments or crossfire — tragic results of urban war, not proof of a state program to target the press.

Likewise, the highly publicized Al-Rashid convoy incident in February 2024 remains disputed: Gaza authorities blamed deliberate Israeli fire, while Israel said many died in a stampede as aid trucks were mobbed. Multiple international outlets report both claims because the forensic record is unclear. When attribution itself is uncertain, proving intentional targeting beyond a reasonable doubt is impossible.

The Global Context: Urban Wars Look Like This

If high civilian casualties in dense cities automatically proved genocide, then we must retroactively indict the U.S. and its allies:

  • Mosul (2016–17): UN and NGO estimates of 9,000–11,000 civilian deaths as ISIS was dislodged.

  • Raqqa (2017): Amnesty documented at least 1,600 civilian deaths during coalition bombing.

Both cities were reduced to rubble. Both campaigns drew sharp criticism for “staggering civilian harm.” Yet neither was called genocide, because the intent was military defeat of an entrenched foe, not extermination of a people.

The Gaza war, however horrifying, fits the same grim pattern of urban conflict — not the legal mold of genocide.

Closing Argument

The opposition offers three pillars: incendiary quotes, tragic numbers, and famine headlines. But incendiary quotes aren’t policy, tragic numbers are contested, and famine is a tragedy of war logistics, not a plan to starve a people.

Strip away the slogans, and the case for genocide collapses. What remains is what Israel itself admits: a devastating, bloody war against an enemy that embeds itself in civilians. That is tragedy enough. But it is not genocide.
excerpt: To accuse Israel of “genocide” is to drain that word of all meaning. Genocide requires intent to exterminate a people — not reckless destruction, not tragic civilian casualties, but a deliberate plan.

author: Natoli Lemessa
title: North Korea Is Socialist… But How?
subtitle: Few countries in the modern world describe themselves as openly “socialist” the way North Korea does. Officially called the Democratic People’s Republic of Korea (DPRK), it has for decades claimed to embody socialism, with its own twist: Juche (self-reliance). Yet when we look beyond slogans, how “socialist” is North Korea in practice — and how does it compare to the textbook version of socialism?
content:
The Foundation: Marxism-Leninism and Juche

North Korea was born in 1948 as a Soviet-backed state. Its first constitution enshrined Marxism-Leninism, pledging to build a classless society based on collective ownership. By the 1970s, Kim Il-sung (the founding leader) declared a more distinct path: Juche ideology.

Juche is often called “Korean socialism,” but it shifts emphasis from internationalist class struggle to national self-reliance. That meant three pillars:

  1. Political independence (no domination by foreign powers)

  2. Economic self-reliance (avoiding dependence on imports or aid)

  3. Military strength (Songun, or “military-first” policy, added later)

This blend — Marxist economics with nationalist flavor — remains the DPRK’s official doctrine.

The Socialist Economy on Paper

In theory, North Korea’s economy fits socialist principles:

  • Collective ownership: Land and factories belong to the state or cooperatives.

  • Central planning: The government issues multi-year economic plans.

  • Distribution by need, not profit: Goods and services are allocated by ration, not by market price.

Data from the UN and the DPRK’s own reports in the 1970s show annual GDP growth rates of 12–15%, briefly making North Korea richer than South Korea. State-led investment in steel, chemicals, and energy helped it industrialize quickly.

By the 1980s, however, cracks emerged. Central planning produced inefficiencies, and self-reliance left the country technologically stagnant. When the Soviet Union collapsed in 1991, subsidies and trade vanished — triggering the devastating famine of the 1990s (the “Arduous March”), in which estimates suggest hundreds of thousands to over a million people died.

The Reality Today: State Socialism with Market Shadows

Officially, North Korea still operates under socialist central planning. But on the ground, reality looks different:

  • Markets (Jangmadang): Informal markets sprang up during the famine and now account for much of the country’s food and consumer goods distribution. Studies by Seoul’s Korea Development Institute estimate that up to 70% of North Korean households rely on markets for survival, not the state ration system.

  • Inequality: A wealthy elite in Pyongyang enjoys imported goods, cars, and apartments, while rural areas remain impoverished. This contradicts socialist ideals of equality.

  • Military-first economics: A huge share of resources goes to the armed forces. The Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (SIPRI) estimates 25% of North Korea’s GDP is spent on the military — the highest ratio in the world.

So while the rhetoric is socialist, the lived reality is closer to a hybrid: a state-controlled command economy with tolerated black markets.

Is It Socialist?

The answer depends on how you define socialism.

  • Yes, by doctrine: North Korea’s constitution still enshrines socialism. The state owns industry, runs collective farms, and rejects capitalist markets in principle.

  • No, by practice: The rise of private markets, extreme inequality, and the military-first priority show a system drifting far from socialism’s promise of egalitarian prosperity.

In truth, North Korea is less “socialist utopia” and more authoritarian state capitalism under socialist branding.

Conclusion

North Korea calls itself socialist — and in structure, it is. The state controls land, factories, and distribution, and the ruling party claims to act in the name of the working class. But the famine, marketization, and the rise of an elite have hollowed out much of that reality.

The better description is this: North Korea is socialist in ideology, militarized in practice, and unequal in reality. Its survival depends less on socialist purity and more on political control, military deterrence, and a careful allowance of markets beneath the surface.
